Actinic Keratoses.advice: Actinic keratoses are rough, scaly patches caused by long-term sun exposure. Protect your skin from UV radiation by wearing sunscreen (SPF 30+), protective clothing, and avoiding direct sun during peak hours. Monitor the lesion regularly because some cases may progress to squamous cell carcinoma.
Actinic Keratoses.doctor_advice: Consult a dermatologist if the lesion grows rapidly, bleeds, or becomes painful. Early treatment prevents potential progression to skin cancer.

Basal Cell Carcinoma.advice: Basal cell carcinoma is the most common form of skin cancer and usually develops due to prolonged sun exposure. Avoid tanning beds, use broad-spectrum sunscreen daily, and regularly check for new or changing skin lesions.
Basal Cell Carcinoma.doctor_advice: Medical evaluation is necessary. Dermatologists may recommend procedures such as cryotherapy, Mohs surgery, or photodynamic therapy.

Benign Keratosis.advice: Benign keratosis lesions are non-cancerous skin growths that commonly appear with aging. Maintain proper skin hygiene and protect skin from excessive sunlight to prevent irritation or worsening.
Benign Keratosis.doctor_advice: Consult a dermatologist if the lesion changes color, size, or texture, as similar appearances can sometimes mimic malignant lesions.

Dermatofibroma.advice: Dermatofibromas are harmless fibrous skin nodules often caused by minor injuries such as insect bites. Avoid scratching the area and maintain skin hygiene.
Dermatofibroma.doctor_advice: Treatment is usually unnecessary unless the lesion becomes painful or cosmetically bothersome. Surgical removal may be recommended in rare cases.

Melanoma.advice: Melanoma is a serious type of skin cancer that develops in pigment-producing cells. Early detection is critical. Follow the ABCDE rule (Asymmetry, Border irregularity, Color variation, Diameter >6mm, Evolving lesions) to monitor suspicious moles.
Melanoma.doctor_advice: Seek immediate medical attention. Dermatologists typically recommend biopsy and possible surgical removal or immunotherapy depending on severity.

Melanocytic Nevus.advice: Melanocytic nevi (moles) are common and usually benign. Avoid excessive sun exposure and monitor moles for unusual changes in shape, size, or color.
medicines: No medication required in most cases
Melanocytic Nevus.doctor_advice: If the mole shows sudden growth, bleeding, itching, or color change, consult a dermatologist to rule out melanoma.

Vascular Lesion.advice: Vascular lesions are skin abnormalities involving blood vessels, such as hemangiomas or angiomas. Most are harmless but should be monitored for growth or bleeding.
Vascular Lesion.doctor_advice: Consult a dermatologist if the lesion enlarges, becomes painful, or causes cosmetic concerns.
